name: Epic FHIR API
what_it_does: Epic's FHIR R4 compliant API for accessing and exchanging electronic health record (EHR) data including patient demographics, clinical observations, medications, and care plans from Epic EHR installations.
use_cases: Retrieving patient clinical data (observations, medications, conditions) for care coordination agents
Querying appointment and scheduling data for patient outreach workflows
Reading diagnostic reports and lab results for clinical decision support
Population health queries across patient cohorts using FHIR bulk export
Integrating Epic EHR data into care management platforms and dashboards
not_for: Facilities not using Epic EHR system
Writing or modifying clinical data (Epic's FHIR write support is limited)
Consumer health apps accessing non-Epic health systems
best_when: You're building clinical applications, care coordination tools, or health analytics that need access to structured EHR data from Epic installations using standard FHIR R4.
avoid_when: The healthcare organization uses Cerner, Athena, or another EHR — Epic FHIR only works with Epic-based systems.

auth.notes: SMART on FHIR OAuth2. Standalone launch (backend services) and EHR launch flows. Backend service apps use asymmetric JWT authentication (private key). Patient-level and system-level scopes. Each Epic installation may configure access differently.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Sandbox (open.epic.com) with synthetic patient data is free
pricing.paid_tiers: No direct pricing — access granted by healthcare organizations via Epic Sandbox or customer agreements
pricing.notes: Epic does not charge developers directly for API access. Healthcare organizations control who can access their Epic instance. Register app at open.epic.com for sandbox testing.

performance.rate_limits: Varies by installation and access level; bulk export has separate throttling

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: FHIR OperationOutcome resources used for errors. Severity and diagnostics fields provided. Some errors are installation-specific and undocumented at the standard level.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: FHIR PUT with explicit resource ID is idempotent. POST (create) is not idempotent. Conditional create with If-None-Exist header provides idempotent creation.
known_agent_gotchas: Each Epic installation is configured independently — app permissions vary per customer deployment
Backend service JWT auth requires careful private key management — each Epic customer has a separate public key registration
FHIR resource IDs are installation-specific — IDs from one Epic instance cannot be used with another
Bulk FHIR export (Group/$export) is async with polling — must handle long-running export jobs
FHIR R4 vs STU3 support varies by Epic version and customer upgrade cycle
Patient identity matching across organizations requires careful deduplication logic
Sandbox synthetic patients (open.epic.com) do not reflect production data complexity
